title: Inside and outside the U.S. Capitol, the fifth anniversary of Jan. 6 reverberates
summary: A small crowd of far-right activists marched on the U.S. Capitol Tuesday, Jan. 6, 2026 in a nonviolent protest. They followed the path of the march five years ago, when rioters attacked the Capitol in an attempt to stop the certification of Joe Biden's presidential election win. (Photo by Ashley Murray/States Newsroom) WASHINGTON — Five years after a pro-Trump mob stormed the U.S. Capitol, the struggle to define the event and assign blame carried on in events across the city Tuesday that remained nonviolent, though still disturbing. A crowd of no more than a few hundred of President Donald Trump’s supporters commemorated the deadly attack with a somewhat subdued march from the Ellipse to the Capitol that was in stark contrast to the riot five years ago. Former national Proud Boys leader Enrique Tarrio looked on as far-right activists celebrating the Jan. 6 Capitol attack marched down Constitution Avenue on Tuesday, Jan. 6, 2026....

title: Op-Ed: From Allende to Maduro – the price of resources and defiance
summary: From Chile to Venezuela, battles over who controls strategic resources have repeatedly shaped regime change, foreign intervention and political fallout across the Americas. When Chilean socialist President Salvador Allende nationalized the copper industry in 1971, he moved to bring foreign-owned copper mines —primarily controlled by US corporations such as Anaconda and Kennecott— under state control through the creation of Codelco (Corporación Nacional del Cobre de Chile/ National Copper Corporation of Chile). With this, Allende directly challenged corporate and US strategic interests tied to the country’s most valuable ex...

title: AST MVC-D: Palmer [AK26001428]
summary: On 01/06/2026 at approximately 1314 hours, AST responded to a report of a 3-vehicle collision on Palmer Wasilla Hwy at Trunk Rd in Palmer. Investigation revealed Bobby King, 83 yoa from Wasilla, was driving his grey 2015 Ford F150 westbound on Palmer Wasilla Hwy and was unable to stop due to icy road conditions. Bobby's vehicle collided into the rear of a 2009 Mercedez Benz ML55, driven by a 17 yoa female of Palmer. The Mercedez Benz ML55 was pushed forward from the impact and collided into the rear of a 2025 white F150 driven by Bruce Hall, 47 yoa of Palmer. No injuries were reported. All individuals were wearing their seatbelts at the time of the collision. All vehicles were driven from the scene.
sourceAttribution: Source: Alaska State Troopers Daily Dispatch

title: Alaska’s senior senator weighs in on public broadcasting, transboundary mining and Peltola’s options
summary: Sen. Lisa Murkowski, R-Alaska, speaks at an Aug. 4, 2025, news conference in her Anchorage office. (Photo by Yereth Rosen/Alaska Beacon) It was an eventful year in national politics, and Alaska’s senior senator was often in the spotlight for bucking the Republican Party or the Trump administration. She sat down with veteran Alaska reporter Nathaniel Herz of the Northern Journal to reflect on the last year and look forward into 2026 — in a conversation that ranges from federal funding for public broadcasting to Alaska’s 2026 U.S. Senate election to transboundary mining. The Alaska Beacon is republishing this edited transcript of their conversation. Nathaniel Herz: Have you seen these t-shirts yet, with this quote from you? Lisa Murkowski: I’m looking at it. What is it? Oh, yeah. It says, ‘What’s woke about the birthday line?’ Right? What’s woke about the Talk of Alaska Merry Christmas greeting (show) today? Nathaniel Herz: My understanding is, the genesis of these t-shirts was that you...

title: Midcycle redistricting deepens America’s divide
summary: Voters line up in the lobby of Anchorage's City Hall on Nov. 4, with most waiting 90 minutes to two hours that morning to cast their ballots at the early voting site there. (Photo by Yereth Rosen/Alaska Beacon) When the Indiana state Senate recently rejected a mid-cycle partisan redistricting of its congressional delegation, it was not only a rebuke to President Donald J. Trump; it also upheld a norm that has guided American democracy for more than a century. Since the early 1900s, states have almost never redrawn congressional maps outside the decennial census, except to comply with court orders or to make minor technical corrections. That restraint has served an important stabilizing function. History suggests that midcycle gerrymanders lead to greater division and polarization, and more volatility in the Congress. And the impact may hit closer to home, if Virginia voters uphold Democratic legislators’ current effort to reshape the state’s political map...
